markdownRemark.html:
Cascading row dataset, e.g.: { name1: dataSet1, name2: dataSet2 }

Property Description Type Default
children Cascading row dataset, e.g.: { name1: dataSet1, name2: dataSet2 } { name: DataSet }
cascadeParams Parameters for cascading queries (record, primaryKey) => object (record, primaryKey) => primaryKey ? record.get(primaryKey) : record.toData()

Basic Usage

There are two ways to get cascading data by setting the children property:

The first is to query the child in real-time when switching the parent record.

The second is to fetch it together with the parent data, where the child data is returned as a field in the API response. In this case, all child data must be returned, and backend pagination cannot be used.

// Child 1
const friendsDS = new DataSet({
  // Setting queryUrl, name, and transport.read will perform the associated query
  queryUrl: '/dataset/user/queries',
  cascadeParams(parent, primaryKey) {
    console.log('cascadeParams', parent.toData(), primaryKey);
    // Parameters for cascading queries (record, primaryKey) => object, passed to the backend
    return {
      __parent: parent.toData(),
    };
  },
  fields: [
    { name: 'name', type: 'string', label: 'Name', required: true },
    { name: 'age', type: 'number', label: 'Age', step: 1 },
  ],
});

// Child 2
const enemyDS = new DataSet({
  autoQuery: false,
  fields: [
    { name: 'name', type: 'string', label: 'Name', required: true },
    { name: 'age', type: 'number', label: 'Age', step: 1 },
  ],
});

// Parent
const userDS = new DataSet({
  primaryKey: 'userid',
  transport: {
    read({ params: { page, pagesize } }) {
      return {
        url: `/dataset/user/page/${pagesize}/${page}`,
      };
    },
  },
  fields: [
    { name: 'name', type: 'string', label: 'Name', required: true },
    { name: 'age', type: 'number', label: 'Age', step: 1 },
  ],
  children: {
    friends: friendsDS,
    // 'other' is a field returned by the backend when querying userDS; the data structure looks like: [{ other: { enemy: [{}, {}, {}] } }]
    'other.enemy': enemyDS,
  },
})

Notes

  1. In parent-child relationships, child operations can only be performed if the parent record has a value.

Applicable Scenarios

  1. Standard header row (cascading)

    1. For a one-to-many header row, link using the header ID. Set the children primaryKey property in the corresponding dataSet. The primary key is used by default in cascading queries inside the component library.
    2. Three-level cascade (the demo below is for a standard header row with three-level cascading).
  2. Special header row
  3. Placeholder row: There is no actual ID association, but you can set the association with the dataSet through children, and cascade query parameters can be customized using the cascadeParams property.
  4. Special header row structure: You can interact between multiple dataSets without using the children property, controlling the data interaction yourself. You can use events like query, load, and submit on the dataSet to facilitate interaction.

Try it out

The following example uses four dataSets: users, users' friends, users' enemies, and friends of users' enemies. You can switch records and click query to see changes in the interface, as well as view events or request parameters in the console.

Click the toData and toJSONData buttons to view the data structure.

Note: The official website uses mock data. In the demo, the friends query returns fixed data and does not return results based on query conditions.
